did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning and deep learning techniques, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and the DenseNet201 model, for skin cancer detection. The methodology involved image preprocessing, segmentation, feature extraction, and classification of skin lesions (including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.) using the ISIC dataset, achieving an accuracy of 89%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a machine learning framework and a mobile application ('Skin Doctor') for skin cancer detection. It details specific methodology including data collection from the ISIC dataset, preprocessing, segmentation, feature extraction using DenseNet201, and model evaluation with reported accuracy metrics (89%). Although published in conference proceedings, it reports new results and a novel system implementation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Detection using Machine Learning Framework with Mobile Application
journal: Proceedings of the 7th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI 2023)
brief_summary: This study addresses the critical need for early skin cancer diagnosis by developing a machine learning framework and a mobile application named 'Skin Doctor'. The system utilizes thermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, which includes lesions such as melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and seborrheic keratosis. The methodology involves preprocessing, segmentation, and feature extraction using the DenseNet201 deep learning model to classify skin lesions. The model was evaluated on the public dataset, achieving an accuracy of 89%. The research concludes by demonstrating the successful implementation of this framework into a mobile app for quick and accessible skin cancer detection.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion categories (Actinic Keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Seborrheic keratosis, and Vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper uses a DenseNet201 convolutional neural network to classify skin lesions into discrete diagnostic categories. The methodology involves feature extraction and stratification of characteristics to distinguish between malignant and benign lesions. The model was evaluated using a confusion matrix and classification report (precision, recall, F1-score) on the ISIC dataset, achieving an accuracy of 89%.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing thermoscopic images of skin lesions from various populations. The paper notes that the ISIC collection includes gold-standard lesion diagnostic information and tens of thousands of images. Specifically, the paper references that ISIC produced 2357 pictures of malignant and benign oncological diseases, and 2144 thermoscopic pictures underwent careful inspection.

use.training: Used for training the DenseNet201 model (approximately 2565 images utilized in the endeavor).
use.validation: Used for validation (training and validation accuracy graphs were provided).
use.testing: Used for testing (test accuracy denotes detection of independent images not used in training).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To extract statistical texture features (contrast, correlation, energy, entropy, homogeneity) for discriminating between non-cancerous and malignant skin lesions.
To represent average pixel intensities, asymmetry of pixel distribution, and peaking of pixel distribution, respectively, as part of the statistical parameters for analysis.
To evaluate classifier performance by counting True Positives, False Positives, False Negatives, and True Negatives.
To assess the accuracy of classification recommendations, with F1-score calculated as the harmonic mean of precision and recall.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, confusion matrices, precision, recall, and F1-score.

ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collabo.dataset_description: Public dataset containing thermoscopic images of skin lesions. Approximately 2565 images were utilized in the endeavor.

split_details.training: Used for training the DenseNet201 model.
split_details.validation: Used for validation (training and validation accuracy graphs provided).
split_details.testing: Used for testing independent images not used in training.

evaluation_metrics.precision: Reported via classification report (specific values not provided in text, referenced as Fig. 7).
evaluation_metrics.recall: Reported via classification report (specific values not provided in text, referenced as Fig. 7).
evaluation_metrics.f1_score: Reported via classification report (specific values not provided in text, referenced as Fig. 7).
evaluation_metrics.confusion_matrix: Reported (referenced as Fig. 6). Specific TP, TN, FP, FN counts not explicitly listed in text.

DenseNet201.notes: The model was trained on approximately 2565 images. The paper states an accuracy of 89% was discovered using the publicly available dataset. Training and validation accuracy/loss graphs were provided (Fig. 4, Fig. 5). Test accuracy denotes detection of independent images.

ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collabo.notes: The paper does not provide a breakdown of accuracy per class or specific numerical values for precision, recall, and F1-score in the text, only referencing figures (Fig. 7) for the classification report. The overall accuracy is stated as 89%.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on the ImageNet database (more than a million images)

architecture_details.description: A CNN with 201 layers. DenseNet refers to convolutional networks that are densely linked. The architecture includes convolutional layers for distinguishing contrast and entropy, and fully connected layers for image classification.

training_details.preprocessing: Segmentation, conversion to grayscale (rgb2gray), downsizing to 224x224x3
training_details.feature_extraction: Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) for texture features (contrast, correlation, energy, entropy, homogeneity) and statistical parameters (mean, skewness, kurtosis)

classification_or_output_head.implementation: Implemented using Keras library in TensorFlow

preprocessing_techniques: Segmentation to remove superfluous hair and disturbance and extract the damaged region/lesion from the background
Grayscale conversion using the rgb2gray function (converting RGB to grayscale to remove hue and saturation data)
Downsizing/Resizing images to 224x224x3 dimensions to manage computational load and memory requirements
Randomization of RGB-channel resource images partitioned into 224, 224, and 3 sections

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Two skin lesion adaptation techniques were established: 1) An artificial spectrum of reddish, yellow, brown, and black pigments was used to adapt images. 2) Modifying a database of healthy and unhealthy hues. (Note: This appears to be from a related work cited in the text, not the primary method of the proposed framework, but is the only augmentation-like technique mentioned).
The functionality of the RGB-channel resource images was partitioned into 224, 224, and 3 sections at randomization throughout the whole region of the RGB-channel resource images.

conclusions: The study successfully developed a machine learning framework and a mobile application named 'Skin Doctor' for the quick and accessible diagnosis of skin cancer.

The proposed system utilizes thermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, preprocessing them via segmentation and feature extraction using Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) and statistical parameters.
The DenseNet201 deep learning model was employed for classification, achieving an overall accuracy of 89% on the public dataset.
The system is capable of classifying skin lesions into categories including Actinic Keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Seborrheic keratosis, and Vascular lesions.

The implementation leverages TensorFlow and Keras libraries, integrated into an Android mobile application to facilitate early detection and potentially reduce mortality rates associated with late-stage diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single public dataset (ISIC) with no external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability.
The reported overall accuracy of 89% lacks granular performance metrics (precision, recall, F1-score per class) in the text, with specific values only referenced in figures.
The dataset size is small (approximately 2565 images), which may be insufficient to robustly train a deep learning model like DenseNet201 for 7 classes without significant overfitting risks.
The paper mentions testing other classifiers (KNN, SVM, Ada boost) in related work but does not provide comparative performance results for these models within the proposed framework.
The mobile application ('Skin Doctor') implementation relies on TensorFlow Lite, but the paper does not report on real-world deployment performance, latency, or user studies.

Preprocessing steps include segmentation to remove hair and background, but the specific algorithm used for segmentation is not detailed, posing a risk of data leakage if segmentation relies on ground truth labels.
The study focuses on image-based classification and does not incorporate clinical metadata (e.g., patient history, lesion location), which are critical for comprehensive dermatological diagnosis.

The paper is a conference proceeding with limited peer review depth compared to journal publications, and the methodology description lacks reproducibility details (e.g., hyperparameters, exact train/test split ratios).
